channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Israel the lonely
item.description: The country is not as isolated as its critics hope, but it is alienating some true friends

item.title: Why China Is Cracking Down on Elite Education
item.description: The campaign is motivated by corruption concerns and an old-fashioned egalitarianism.

item.title: What Does Putin Want From Xi?
item.description: Russia seeks to resolve several trade issues during this week’s summit. But China holds the cards.

item.title: Chanel’s creative revival is paying off
item.description: The world’s second-biggest luxury label is turning heads again

item.title: Investors fear another surge in inflation
item.description: So why aren’t more buying inflation-protected bonds?

item.title: Washington Might Be Ready to Bargain With Beijing
item.description: The United States may be accepting the reality of Chinese power.

item.title: Washington Might Be Ready to Bargain With Beijing
item.description: The United States may be accepting the reality of Chinese power.

item.title: A new Ebola outbreak could be the worst in a decade
item.description: Medics know how to respond. But aid cuts, war and the lack of a vaccine make it difficult

item.title: Europe’s secret Plan B to replace NATO
item.description: How NATO’s European members plan to fight if America quits

item.title: Can Armenia’s Democracy Prevail?
item.description: Unconditional Western support for the incumbent prime minister could backfire.

item.title: Middle East Dispatch: What Binyamin Netanyahu’s opponents won’t say
item.description: The campaign season for a possible Israeli election has already started, writes Josie Delap, our Middle East editor

item.title: Three Concrete Steps to Advance Palestinian Freedom
item.description: Legal reform and structural change may lack flair, but they can improve the situation on the ground.

item.title: The Geopolitical Circumstances of Japan-South Korea Rapprochement
item.description: The latest Lee-Takaichi summit highlighted not only their warm personal rapport but their priorities: energy and economic security.

item.title: What Does a ‘Constructive’ China-US Relationship Mean for India?
item.description: As things stand, Trump’s hopes for a G-2 remain elusive. This leaves New Delhi with enough room to maneuver diplomatically.

item.title: Tibet’s Erasure From the Trump-Xi Summit Is a Huge Win for China
item.description: At least since the 1980s, American leaders visiting Beijing were traditionally expected to raise the Tibetan issue during bilateral meetings. Not anymore.

item.title: Sustainable Economies Will Own the Future
item.description: The COVID-19 pandemic and the wars in Ukraine and Iran have reframed the issue of sustainability, which is now as much about sovereignty and economic security as it is about planetary health. Countries and companies that fail to recognize this have everything to lose in the coming years.

item.title: The US Is Quietly Torpedoing Its Relationship With Pacific Island Partners
item.description: The Trump administration’s actions – or lack thereof – in its dealings with the FAS are undermining U.S. interests in the Pacific.

item.title: A Blueprint for Britain’s Revival
item.description: There are five big policy-related problems holding back the UK economy. Any politician who tackles them in earnest would benefit from a bond- and equity-market rally, a construction boom, higher business and consumer confidence, and the political dividends that come with broad-based gains to voters' standard of living.

item.title: Why Kazakhstan Wants the Turkic World to Be a Tech Hub, Not a Military Bloc
item.description: Tokayev made this logic explicit during the informal OTS summit when he warned that countries that fail to adapt to technological transformation risk being left behind.

item.title: China’s Plan for Winning the AI Race Hinges on the Token Economy, Not Chips
item.description: China is translating the old concept of “encircling the cities from the countryside” for the digital age.

item.title: The Democrats have a chance to win the Senate. Will they blow it?
item.description: Much depends on what happens in places like Michigan

item.title: Japanese eels have two types of sperm
item.description: Understanding why could help boost their numbers

item.title: India Is Hosting the 4th India-Africa Forum Summit: Why Now and What to Expect?
item.description: For the first time in 11 years, African leaders and multilateral organizations will convene in the Indian capital.

item.title: The Middle Corridor’s Energy Dimension: A New Phase in Turkiye-Kazakhstan Ties
item.description: Kazakh oil and its connection to the Middle Corridor are placing the Turkiye-Kazakhstan relationship in a more critical position within Eurasian energy geopolitics.

item.title: The Real Key Moment for Taiwan Comes After the Trump-Xi Summit
item.description: Trump’s summit with Xi deferred the real Taiwan test: whether U.S. arms sales to Taipei will preserve credible support under strategic ambiguity, and how far Beijing will go to enforce its red line.

item.title: Trump’s Trip to Beijing: When Style Became Substance
item.description: Trump was looking for a breakthrough, but not necessarily in the form of major trade deals. Trump’s mission was to insert charm and his own personality into the relationship with Xi.

item.title: Asian Banks Hide Behind a Coal Funding Smokescreen
item.description: People across Southeast Asian countries – Indonesia, Malaysia, and Singapore – want bank funding to end for all companies, including coal, that are making climate change worse.

item.title: Iran Is Pressing Its Strategic Advantage
item.description: Military and economic coercion usually costs the coercer as well as the target. But by threatening undersea cables beneath the Strait of Hormuz, Iran may have found a way to impose heavy costs on the US government and private sector while generating revenue for itself—at least in the short term.

item.title: Even by Trumpian standards, a $1.8bn fund for friends is bad
item.description: The president threatened to sue his own administration, and will hand out the pay-off

item.title: The World Keeps Asking Iran the Wrong Question
item.description: Even before the Islamic Republic, the country has always wanted the same thing.

item.title: The World Keeps Asking Iran the Wrong Question
item.description: Even before the Islamic Republic, the country has always wanted the same thing.

item.title: Cartographic Justice for Africa
item.description: For centuries, the Mercator map has distorted Africa’s size, shaping perceptions of the continent's strategic significance and economic prospects. Adopting more accurate map projections is neither technically complex nor prohibitively expensive, yet the symbolic and material consequences could be profound.

item.title: The Indonesian Air Force’s Turkish Turn
item.description: Jakarta has recently signed onto several major Turkish aerospace industry programs with significant implications for the future of its airpower and domestic defense industry.

item.title: Indonesian Rupiah Falls to New Low as Global Oil Prices Surge
item.description: The slump was accompanied by a 4 percent drop in Jakarta's main stock index, which is under close scrutiny from the global index provider MSCI.

item.title: The Myth of Zero Enrichment
item.description: Tehran regards Washington’s demand as tantamount to unconditional surrender, but there may yet be a way forward.

item.title: The Myth of Zero Enrichment
item.description: Tehran regards Washington’s demand as tantamount to unconditional surrender, but there may yet be a way forward.

item.title: Orban’s Fall and Europe’s Rise
item.description: The dawn of a strange new European consensus.

item.title: The Not-So-Quiet American
item.description: Pope Leo takes the Vatican where Francis could not.

item.title: The Not-So-Quiet American
item.description: Pope Leo takes the Vatican where Francis could not.

item.title: How Malaysia’s Semiconductor Industry Can Thrive in an Era of Strategic Competition
item.description: The country occupies a uniquely strategic position due to its proven ability to balance competing geopolitical and economic forces.

item.title: Philippine Senate Kicks Off Impeachment Trial of Vice President Sara Duterte
item.description: Whatever the trial's outcome, the political war between Duterte and President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. is unlikely to come to a conclusive end.

item.title: With an ‘Obstacle’ Gone, Will the Indian Government Finally Seal the Teesta Deal With Bangladesh?
item.description: An agreement to share the waters of the Teesta River will not go down well in northern West Bengal.

item.title: Farmers Endure Militarization in the Philippines’ ‘Massacre Capital’
item.description: Soldiers claim 19 rebel deaths in “legitimate encounter” last month, but questions linger over the killings in Negros, which included a student leader, a journalist, a local farmer, and others.

item.title: Southeast Asia’s Mandalas of Multialignment
item.description: Prashanth Parameswaran joins the Asia Geopolitics podcast to explain how multialignment shapes Southeast Asia’s foreign policy and the region’s strategic importance.
